mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Japan on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the far east. It is an island country off the east coast of the Asian mainland 🌏

mapQuestions.question: How many islands does Japan have?
mapQuestions.answer: Around 14,000 islands. Four big ones make up most of the country: Honshu (the biggest, where Tokyo is), Hokkaido, Kyushu and Shikoku.

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Japan?
mapQuestions.answer: Tokyo. The wider Tokyo area has about 37 million people, making it the largest urban area in the world.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in Japan?
mapQuestions.answer: Mount Fuji, 3,776 metres. It is an active volcano (last erupted in 1707) and is the most famous symbol of Japan.

mapQuestions.question: Why is Japan called the "Land of the Rising Sun"?
mapQuestions.answer: Because it sits at the eastern edge of Asia. From the Asian mainland, the sun looks like it rises out of Japan. The Japanese name for Japan, "Nippon" or "Nihon", literally means "origin of the sun".

foods.name: Sushi
foods.description: Bite-sized blocks of vinegared rice topped with raw fish (nigiri) or rolled in seaweed with fillings inside (maki). At a conveyor-belt place, plates whiz past, you grab what you want.

foods.name: Ramen
foods.description: Wheat noodles in a steaming bowl of broth that has been simmered for hours, with toppings like soft-boiled egg, pork slices, spring onion and a sheet of nori seaweed.

foods.name: Tempura
foods.description: Prawns and vegetables dipped in light batter and deep-fried until they crackle. Best eaten right out of the fryer, dipped in salt or a soy-ginger sauce.

foods.name: Mochi
foods.description: Chewy, stretchy little rice cakes made from steamed and pounded glutinous rice. Often filled with sweet red bean paste, sometimes wrapped around ice cream. Unique texture.

foods.name: Yakitori
foods.description: Skewers of chicken (every part, including bits you would not expect) grilled over charcoal, brushed with sweet soy. A classic snack at a smoky little izakaya pub.

foods.name: Okonomiyaki
foods.description: A savoury pancake of cabbage, batter and your choice of fillings, cooked on a hot iron griddle and topped with mayonnaise, brown sauce and dancing bonito flakes. From Osaka.

scavengerItems.label: A vending machine in a totally random place (Japan has over 4 million of them)
A red torii gate marking the entrance to a Shinto shrine
A conveyor belt of sushi rolling past your table
A cherry blossom tree (if you are here in spring) or a cherry blossom design on something
A "maneki-neko" lucky waving cat in a shop window
A perfectly tidy row of shoes lined up outside a doorway
A cute mascot character on a bus, train or roadworks sign
A vending machine selling hot drinks in cans
Someone wearing a kimono or yukata in the street
A traditional Japanese castle or pagoda
A robot doing a job a human could do (taking orders, cleaning a floor)

geoMatches.description: The mega-capital. Neon-lit Shibuya crossing, calm temple gardens, vending machines on every corner, and the world's busiest train stations.
The old imperial capital. 1,600 Buddhist temples, 400 Shinto shrines, bamboo forests, geisha quarters, and traditional wooden houses.
The street-food capital. Loud, friendly, with a giant mechanical crab outside a restaurant and the best okonomiyaki and takoyaki in Japan.
The wild northern island. Snow festivals, hot springs (onsen), brown bears in the mountains, and creamy local dairy.
A city destroyed by an atomic bomb in 1945, rebuilt as a city of peace. The Peace Memorial Park has a museum, a flame that has burned since 1964, and the skeleton of the dome that survived the blast.
A near-perfect cone-shaped volcano on the main island, the tallest mountain in Japan. People climb it overnight in summer to watch the sunrise from the top.

senses.smell: Soy sauce on grilled rice cakes, cedar at a shrine, hot dashi broth, cherry blossoms in spring, tatami straw mats, the herbal steam of an onsen…
senses.hear: "Irasshaimase!" (welcome!) every time you walk into a shop, train chimes at every station, cicadas screaming in summer, temple bells, the soft slide of paper doors…
senses.taste: Salty soy, sweet teriyaki, the cool clean taste of fresh sushi rice, the bitterness of matcha powder, sticky mochi, the burn of wasabi up the nose…
senses.feel: Cool tatami mats underfoot (shoes off!), the rumble of the bullet train, hot onsen water on tired legs, the soft brush of a paper sliding door, summer humidity…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why are there vending machines on every corner?
stories.body: Japan has more than 4 million vending machines, more per person than anywhere else on earth. They sell drinks (hot and cold from the same machine!), umbrellas, ice cream, ramen, hot soup, eggs, even fresh apples. They sit on remote country roads with nothing else around. Why so many? Japan is very safe so nobody smashes them, the population is ageing so machines are cheaper than staff, and Japanese people love convenience and quality. The machines never break, never overcharge, never run out. They are a tiny perfect example of how Japan works.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you could put one vending machine on every street corner near your home, selling something weird and useful, what would it sell?

stories.question: Why is the whole country obsessed with pink flowers for two weeks?
stories.body: Every spring, Japan goes pink. Cherry blossom trees (sakura) burst into bloom for just a week or two, and the entire country stops to look. Friends and families gather under the trees with picnic blankets, snacks and (for grown-ups) sake, in a tradition called "hanami", flower-viewing. Forecasters track exactly when the blossoms will open in each city, like a weather report. The flowers are precious because they fall so quickly, a reminder that beautiful things do not last. The first weather forecast Japanese people obsess over each year is not the weather, it is the blossoms.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Is there something in your year you always look forward to, that is over very quickly?

stories.question: Why does everyone keep bowing?
stories.body: In Japan, people bow instead of shake hands or hug. There are different bows for different situations: a tiny nod for hello, a deeper bow for thank you, a very deep bow for an apology or great respect. Children learn the different bows at school. Even people on the phone bow when they are talking, even though the person on the other end cannot see. Shop assistants bow when you walk in, train conductors bow when they leave the carriage, sumo wrestlers bow before they fight. It is a way of saying "I see you, I respect you" without saying anything at all.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your family invented a special greeting just for you, what would it be?

stories.question: Why is one Japanese city now a city of peace?
stories.body: On 6 August 1945, near the end of World War 2, the United States dropped an atomic bomb on Hiroshima. It was the first time a nuclear weapon was ever used in a war. The bomb destroyed almost everything within 2km of where it landed and killed about 140,000 people. A second bomb was dropped on Nagasaki three days later, and Japan surrendered. Today Hiroshima has been rebuilt as a peaceful, green, modern city, and the skeleton of the dome at the centre is preserved exactly as it was that morning. Every year on 6 August, paper lanterns are floated down the river at sunset to remember the dead and to ask the world never to use such a weapon again.
stories.thinkingQuestion: After everything that happened, the people of Hiroshima decided their message to the world would be "peace". What do you think you would have wanted to do in their place?

stories.question: Why are Japanese trains the most punctual on earth?
stories.body: The shinkansen, the bullet trains, run at up to 320 km/h and connect almost every major Japanese city. Their average yearly delay is around 36 seconds. If a train is even one minute late, the conductor will apologise to the passengers over the speaker. They have never had a single fatal crash from a derailment or collision in their entire 60-year history. There are special cleaning crews (the "shichi-fun-kan girls") who turn each train around in 7 minutes between stops. The whole system runs on absolute precision: train doors line up with marked spots on the platform within centimetres, every single time.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Japan so far?
Have you tried sushi with raw fish? What did you actually think?
What is one Japanese habit you would like to take home with you?
Did you find a really weird vending machine? What was in it?
Where has felt the most "Japanese" so far, in a way you cannot quite explain?
Japan is one of the most orderly countries on earth. What do you think would be hard about living somewhere where everyone follows so many rules?

wordSearch.hint: Japanese foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Cat-cafe cat
animals.description: Japan loves cats. Cat cafés and "cat islands" (like Aoshima) exist for this reason.

animals.name: Sika deer of Nara
animals.description: Around 1,000 freely-roaming sacred deer in Nara Park bow to you (then mug you for crackers).

animals.name: Koi carp
animals.description: In every temple pond, garden pond and shrine pool. Some are 100+ years old.

animals.name: Common crane
animals.description: On 1,000 yen notes and at Hokkaido marshes — the red-crowned crane is national symbol.

animals.name: Snow monkey (Japanese macaque)
animals.description: In Nagano they bathe in hot springs in the snow. Adorable.

animals.name: Humpback whale
animals.description: On migration past Okinawa in winter and Hokkaido in summer.
